flashcards.front: What is the main difference between BFS and DFS in terms of search strategy?
flashcards.back: BFS explores all nodes at current depth before going deeper (level-by-level), while DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking.

flashcards.front: What data structure does BFS use for exploration?
flashcards.back: BFS uses a Queue (FIFO - First In First Out) to manage the frontier nodes.

flashcards.front: What data structure does DFS use for exploration?
flashcards.back: DFS uses a Stack (LIFO - Last In First Out) or recursion to manage the frontier nodes.

flashcards.front: Is BFS complete? Why or why not?
flashcards.back: Yes, BFS is complete. It will always find a solution if one exists in a finite state space.

flashcards.front: Is BFS optimal? Under what conditions?
flashcards.back: Yes, BFS is optimal when all edges have equal cost (unweighted graph). It finds the shortest path in terms of number of edges.

flashcards.front: Is DFS complete? What is its limitation?
flashcards.back: DFS is not complete in infinite state spaces (can get stuck in infinite depth). It may also get stuck in loops.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of BFS?
flashcards.back: O(b^d) where b is branching factor and d is depth of shallowest goal node.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of BFS?
flashcards.back: O(b^d) - needs to store all frontier and explored nodes.

flashcards.front: What is Hill Climbing classified as - informed or uninformed search?
flashcards.back: Hill Climbing is an informed search technique (a greedy local search algorithm) as it uses heuristic information.

flashcards.front: What is the main drawback of simple Hill Climbing?
flashcards.back: It can get stuck at local maxima (peaks that are not the global maximum), plateaus, or ridges.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing and Simple Hill Climbing?
flashcards.back: Simple Hill Climbing chooses the first better neighbor; Steepest-Ascent evaluates all neighbors and chooses the best one.

flashcards.front: What is Random Restart Hill Climbing?
flashcards.back: A variant that runs multiple hill climbing searches from random initial states to overcome local maxima problems.

flashcards.front: What is a complete search algorithm?
flashcards.back: A search algorithm is complete if it guarantees finding a solution when one exists.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Tree Search and Graph Search versions?
flashcards.back: Graph Search maintains a CLOSED list (explored set) to avoid revisiting nodes; Tree Search does not, potentially exploring same states multiple times.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of DFS?
flashcards.back: O(bm) where b is branching factor and m is maximum depth of the search tree.
